pages_preview.text_preview: P2 Onboarding email SUBJECT: Your [replication/reproduction] of $$AUTHOR$$ (20XX) Greetings $$LAB$$, Thank you for your interest in collaborating with the SCORE project! We are excited for you to join this initiative. These slides contain a summary of the timeline and process for completing a SCORE replication, including: ● an overview of the SCORE project at large ● an introduction to the OSF platform and practice of preregistration ● logistical information regarding project timeline, budgets, and agreements ● ethics review requirements for DoD-funded research This last point is worth highlighting because this process may differ from your typical research ethics review and require additional planning on your part. As we are funded by the U.S. Department of Defense, collaborators who are requesting study funds must get approval from a secondary ethics review through a federal Human Research Protection Office (HRPO) following local approval by an IRB or equivalent research ethics committee. Please take a moment to review the HRPO documents available in this private OSF component, starting with the "IRB instructions." It will be important for you to communicate with your IRB early on to ensure those standards are met. You will also need to provide documents for everything listed in the "HRPO Checklist," and complete the additional CITI training. Please complete your IRB application and send it to us to review as soon as possible before submitting to your IRB. At the very latest, we need your IRB application by [replace this with your due date]. Finally, the Center for Open Science (COS) will provide you with funding for costs associated with your study. To that end, please send us a budget for your project as soon as possible. Note that budgets cannot include overhead, and budgets which exceed $10,000 USD will only be approved on a selective basis. You can see the schedule of payments here as a guide to distinguish costs in terms of HSR (e.g. participant fees) vs non-HSR (e.g., new materials, software licenses, etc.). From here, your immediate next steps for starting your project are: 1. Review the IRB instructions documents and agreement forms available here, and send us your IRB materials as soon as possible.

2. Send us a budget proposal which outlines the breakdown of your study costs by the schedule of payments here. 3. If you are not already familiar with the platform, or just want to learn more about it, you can find guides to using OSF here. 4. Begin completing the preregistration form available here. Please let us know if you have any questions about the information detailed in this message, and we look forward to getting you started on your work with SCORE! Best, COS SCORE Team --- Confidentiality disclaimer: The work you produce for SCORE will help assess how well experts and algorithms can predict the reproducibility of a large set of claims. The integrity of the assessment requires that the research teams generating those predictions not know which specific claims we will be producing replications and reproductions of. We ask that you not publicly share which study you are working on until all results from the SCORE project are made public. Similarly, while we encourage you to engage with the other teams efforts to predict the reproducibility of claims, we ask that you do not participate in those efforts for this paper.

P1 Onboarding emails [for ref] This email is designed to be used as the response to notice that a lab has been sourced. In cases where there has already been some correspondence (e.g., answering some pending questions from the lab first), a version of this email can be sent that excludes anything that has already been addressed and softens the tone if it has become less formal during previous interactions. ************************************************************************************************************* SUBJECT: Your [replication/reproduction] of $$AUTHOR$$ (20XX) Greetings $$LAB$$, Thank you for your interest in collaborating with the SCORE project! We are excited for you to join this initiative. Your project will officially start when you attend one of our onboarding webinars, where a Project Coordinator will discuss the project at large, general expectations for your deliverables, and the ways that the SCORE team at the Center for Open Science are available to help along the way. Please respond to the webinar invitation as soon as possible and register for the earliest webinar you’re able to attend. Prior to the onboarding webinar please review the rest of this email. Your next steps for starting your project are summarized at the end of this message. The work you produce for SCORE will help assess how well experts and algorithms can predict the reproducibility of a large set of claims. The integrity of the assessment requires that the research teams generating those predictions not know which specific claims we will be producing replications and reproductions of. We ask that you not publicly share which study you are working on until all results from the SCORE project are made public. Similarly, while we encourage you to engage with the other teams efforts to predict the reproducibility of claims, we ask that you do not participate in those efforts for this paper. A key part of the process is storing all of your study materials--including publicly available data, analysis scripts, and the final report--on your OSF project page. We encourage you to make as many of those materials as possible open to the public, recognizing that some materials may need to stay private. If you do not already have access to your OSF project, please request access so we can add you as a collaborator. For additional assistance, you will find some user guides to OSF here. The preregistration for your study has been or will be started by one of the COS research scientists and is available for you to begin working on here. [as applicable: I have shared it

with you through an editable link for now as this email does not appear to be associated with a Google account; please let me know if you have a Google account I can use to add you as an editing collaborator.] You should complete each section of the preregistration form based on the content from the original paper, any materials from the original study (if available, these are provided through a view-only link located in your OSF project’s Wiki), and your own judgment based on best practices in the field whenever the original paper and materials are not clear. We encourage you to reference these general guidelines for a strong preregistration. Please start working on the preregistration in the google doc provided so we can easily collaborate. Upon completion of the preregistration and its approval by an external review panel, you will be granted an award of $500. As we are funded by the Department of Defense, collaborators who are requesting study funds must get approval from their own IRB, and a secondary ethics review through a federal Human Research Protection Office (HRPO). Please take a moment to review the HRPO documents available in this private OSF component, starting with the "IRB instructions." These documents outline the parts of this process that may differ from your typical research ethics review and require action on your part. It will be important for you to communicate with your IRB early on to ensure those standards are met. You will also need to provide documents for everything listed in the "HRPO Checklist," and complete the additional CITI training. Please complete your IRB application and send it to us to review as soon as possible. At the very latest, we need you to submit your IRB application by January 10, 2020. Finally, the Center for Open Science (COS) will provide you with funding for costs associated with your study. Therefore we ask that you please send us a budget for your project as soon as possible. Note that budgets cannot include overhead, and budgets which exceed $10,000 USD are subject to an additional level of scrutiny by our federal funder and will only be approved on a selective basis. Feel free to use this template as a guide for how to detail the costs associated with each phase of data collection, breaking down line items by materials/personnel/etc. between round 1 and round 2 of data collection. A portion of the non-HSR funds can be released after submission to the local IRB while the remainder of funds for HSR (e.g. participant fees) will be released after HRPO approval. Final payment will be awarded upon completion of the project and submission of a final report by June 2020. In summary, as this email covers a fair amount of information, your next steps are listed below: 1. If you haven’t already, sign up for an onboarding webinar here. 2. Review the IRB instructions documents and agreement forms available here, and send us your IRB materials as soon as possible. 3. Send us a budget proposal by using this template as a guide for how to break down line items by materials/personnel/etc. between round 1 and round 2 of data collection. If you won’t require funding to conduct your study, please just let us know. 4. If you have not already been added as a collaborator, request access to your OSF project here.

5. Begin completing the preregistration form available here. Please let us know if you have any questions about the information detailed in this message, and we look forward to getting you started on your work with SCORE! Best, Bri, Olivia, and Zach ************************************************************************************************************* This email is designed to be used as the followup to the onboarding call, attaching the presentation. It should start a new thread with the lab. ************************************************************************************************************* SUBJECT: Next steps for your replication of $$AUTHOR$$ (20XX) Greetings/Hi $$LAB$$, Thank you for attending the onboarding webinar today, and for taking the first step towards starting a [replication/reproduction] as part of the SCORE project! If you have any remaining questions about the content of the call that were not addressed earlier, please feel free to respond with them here. As was discussed in the webinar, you will be responsible for producing the following deliverables over the course of your project: ● A preregistered plan to replicate the finding, which will be shared with an external review panel and the original study’s author(s) for their input ● IRB documentation from your home institution ● Approval from the Department of Defense Human Research Protection Office (HRPO), with assistance from COS staff ● A script (or otherwise reproducible description of your analysis) that cleans and transforms the most raw form of your data possible, which outputs an analytic dataset ● A script (or otherwise reproducible description of your analysis) that takes the analytic dataset and produces the results of your study ● A final report of your findings The other project coordinators and I are here to generally facilitate the progress of your work. We will directly respond to the majority of your requests or connect your with resources to guide you. When domain expertise for your protocol is necessary, we will bring in the research scientist associated with your project for targeted feedback.

If you have not already, please take a moment to review the documents available in this private OSF component; starting with the "IRB instructions", these documents outline the parts of this process that may differ from your typical research ethics review and require action on your part. In the same OSF component, please also review the "EXHIBIT A" and the “Center for Open Science Agreement for Services” documents. These documents provide information about project deliverables, the budget, and other logistics, and you’ll be asked to complete and sign these at a later date. Finally, please submit the following tax and bank forms to SCOREaward@cos.io to ensure payment of the financial awards. We are able to process payments to either a personal account or the appropriate office at your institution; in the case of the latter, feel free to pass these forms on to that office and put them in touch with us if they have any questions. Please return one of the following, which we are required to collect for tax purposes: ● For U.S. Citizens and entities or residents living in the U.S., please fill out Form W-9. ● For prize recipients who are not U.S. citizens or U.S. residents, please fill out sections I and III of Form W-8. ● If you are a student in the United States on a visa and not required to file the W-8 form, please provide the Form 8843. Please return one of the following based on recipient bank location , as awards will be paid via bank transfer : ● Domestic Wire Transfer Form (U.S.) ● International Wire Transfer Form (Non-U.S.) Your immediate next steps following this email are to: 1. Develop your IRB application and send it to us as soon as it’s ready. Use the documents contained here to ensure that your IRB materials meet the necessary standards for the federal ethics review that follows local IRB approval. 2. At the same time, develop the draft of your preregistration and alert the coordination team when it is ready for review. 3. Send us your budget proposal, using this template as a guide for how to break down line items by materials/personnel/etc. between round 1 and round 2 of data collection. 4. Submit one tax form and one bank wiring form to SCOREaward@cos.io. Attached here you will find the presentation that was used to guide the webinar. Please let me know if you have any questions about your project or the information detailed above, and once again welcome to SCORE! Best,
